concept:
Tested 1 ? What is the bias introduced into a study when a clinician is aware of what type of treatment his patient received? Observational bias
What is the bias introduced when screening detects a disease earlier and thus lengthens the time from diagnosis to death? Lead-time bias
If you want to know if race affects the infant mortality rate but most of the variation in infant mortality is predicted by socioeconomic status, then socioeconomic status is what? A confounding variable
True positives divided by the number of patients with the disease is called: "Sensitivity". Sensitive tests have few false negatives and are used to rule out a disease.
PPD reactivity is used as a screening test for TB because most people with TB (except those who are anergic) will have a positive PPD. Is this highly sensitive or specific? Highly sensitive
Do chronic diseases like systemic lupus erythematosus SLE have a higher prevalence or incidence? Higher prevalence Higher incidence
Do epidemics such as influenza have a higher prevalence or incidence? Higher incidence Higher prevalence Neither
Does a cross sectional survey measure incidence or prevalence? Prevalence Incidence Neither
Does a case-control study measure incidence of prevalence? Neither Prevalence Incidence
Describe a test that consistently gives identical results but the results are wrong? High reliability, low validity Low reliability, high validity Low reliability, low validity High reliability, high validity
What is the difference between a cohort and a case-control study? Cohort studies are prospective and can be used to calculate relative risk (RR) and incidence. Case-control studies are retrospective and can be used to calculate an odds ratio (OR).
What is the attributable risk? Incidence rate (IR) of disease in exposed
- Incidence rate of disease in unexposed
What is the relative risk? Incidence rate (IR) of a disease in a population exposed to a particular factor divided by IR of those not exposed to the factor
What is the odds ratio? Likelihood of exposure to a risk factor in individual with and without the disease
What is the number needed to treat? 1 per incidence
In which patients do you initiate colorectal cancer screening early? Patients with:
Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)
Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP)/Hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC)
First degree relatives with adenomatous polyps (less than 60 years old)
Colorectal cancer
What is the most common cancer in men and the most common cause of death from cancer in men?
Most common:Prostate cancer
Most fatalities:Lung cancer
What is percentage of cases with one standard deviation (SD) of the mean? Two SDs? Three SDs?
1 SD:68%
2 SDs:95.5%
3 SDs:99.7%
What is the definition of "birth rate"? Number of live births per 1000 population
What is the definition of "fertility rate"? Number of live births per 1000 women 15-44 years old
What is the definition of "mortality rate"? Number of deaths per 1000 population
What is the definition of "neonatal mortality rate"? Number of deaths from birth to 28 days per 1000 live births
What is the definition of "postnatal mortality rate"? Number of deaths from 28 days to one year per 1000 live births
What is the definition of "infant mortality"? Neonatal mortality plus postnatal mortality
What is the definition of "fetal mortality rate"? Number of deaths from 20 weeks' gestation to birth per 1000 total births
What is the definition of "perinatal mortality"? Number of deaths from 20 weeks' gestation to one month of life per 1000 total births
What is the definition of "maternal mortality"? Number of deaths during pregnancy to 90 days postpartum per 100,000 live births
